title: The Bards Who Lived At Manly
content:
آ آ آ آ The camp of high-class spielers,
آ آ آ آ Who sneered in summer dress,
آ آ آ آ And doo-dah dilettante,
آ آ آ آ And scornful ï؟½venusesï؟½,
آ آ آ آ House agents, and storekeepers,
آ آ آ آ All eager they to ï؟½bleedï؟½,
آ آ آ آ The bards who tackled Manly,
آ آ آ آ Were plucky bards indeed!

آ آ آ آ With shops that feared to trust them,
آ آ آ آ And pubs that looked askance;
آ آ آ آ And prigs who read their verses,
آ آ آ آ But gave them not a glance;,
آ آ آ آ When all were vain and selfish,
آ آ آ آ And editors were hard,
آ آ آ آ The bard that stuck to Manly
آ آ آ آ Was sure a mighty bard.

آ آ آ آ What mattered floors were barren,
آ آ آ آ And windows curtainless,
آ آ آ آ And our life seemed to others
آ آ آ آ But blackguard recklessness?
آ آ آ آ We wore our clothes for comfort,
آ آ آ آ We earned our bread alway,
آ آ آ آ And beer and good tobacco
آ آ آ آ Came somehow every day.

آ آ آ آ Came kindred souls to Manly,
آ آ آ آ Outsiders that we knew,
آ آ آ آ And with them scribes and artists,
آ آ آ آ And low comedians too;
آ آ آ آ And sometimes bright girl writers,
آ آ آ آ Called ï؟½Tommyï؟½, ï؟½Jackï؟½, or ï؟½Patï؟½,
آ آ آ آ (Though each one had a sweetheart
آ آ آ آ The rest knew nought of that).

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas not the paltry village
آ آ آ آ We honoured unaware,
آ آ آ آ Or welcome warm, or friendship,
آ آ آ آ Or ï؟½toneï؟½ that took us there;
آ آ آ آ We longed to sing for mankind,
آ آ آ آ Where heavenï؟½s breath was free
آ آ آ آ We only sought the grandeur
آ آ آ آ Of sea-cliff, sands and sea.

آ آ آ آ And we were glad at Manly,
آ آ آ آ All unaware of ï؟½swellsï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Of doctors and of nurses,
آ آ آ آ And private hospitals;
آ آ آ آ With little fear of bailiffs,
آ آ آ آ And great contempt for greed,
آ آ آ آ The bards who lived at Manly,
آ آ آ آ They were a healthy breed.

آ آ آ آ Oh! moonlit nights at Manly,
آ آ آ آ When all the world was fair!
آ آ آ آ In shirts and turned-up trousers
آ آ آ آ We larked like big boys there.
آ آ آ آ Oh! glorious autumn mornings,
آ آ آ آ The gold and green and blue,
آ آ آ آ We ï؟½strippedï؟½ as well as any,
آ آ آ آ And swam as strongly too.

آ آ آ آ The artist had a missus,
آ آ آ آ Who rather loved the wretch,
آ آ آ آ And so for days together
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d stay at home and sketch.
آ آ آ آ And then, I fear ï؟½twas only
آ آ آ آ When things were getting tight,
آ آ آ آ The bards would shun each other,
آ آ آ آ And hump themselves, and write.

آ آ آ آ When bailiffs came to Manly
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d find no ï؟½sticksï؟½ to take,
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½d welcome them as brothers,
آ آ آ آ Their grimy hands weï؟½d shake;
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½d send for beer in billies,
آ آ آ آ And straightway send for more,
آ آ آ آ And bailiff nights in Manly
آ آ آ آ Were merry nights of yore.

آ آ آ آ There are some things that landlords
آ آ آ آ And law canï؟½t do at all:
آ آ آ آ They could not take the pictures
آ آ آ آ We painted on the wall;
آ آ آ آ They could not take the table,
آ آ آ آ The table was a door;
آ آ آ آ They could not take the bedsteads,
آ آ آ آ The beds were on the floor.

آ آ آ آ The door of some old stable,
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½d borrowed for a drink,
آ آ آ آ A page of rhymes and sketches,
آ آ آ آ And stained with beer and ink;
آ آ آ آ A dead hand drew the portraits,
آ آ آ آ And, say, should I be shamed,
آ آ آ آ To seek it out in Manly
آ آ آ آ And get the old door framed?

آ آ آ آ They left the masterpieces
آ آ آ آ The artist dreamed of long;
آ آ آ آ They could not take the gardens
آ آ آ آ From Victor Daleyï؟½s song;
آ آ آ آ They left his summer islands
آ آ آ آ And fairy ships at sea,
آ آ آ آ They could not take my mountains
آ آ آ آ And western plains from me.

آ آ آ آ One bailiff was our brother,
آ آ آ آ No better and no worse,
آ آ آ آ And, oh! the yarns he told us
آ آ آ آ To put in prose and verse,
آ آ آ آ And sorry we to lose him,
آ آ آ آ And sorry he to go,
آ آ آ آ (Oh! skeletons of Pottï؟½s Point,
آ آ آ آ How many things we know)!

آ آ آ آ The very prince of laughter,
آ آ آ آ With brains and sympathy;
آ آ آ آ And with us on the last night
آ آ آ آ He spent his bailiffï؟½s fee.
آ آ آ آ He banished Durkinï؟½s gruffness,
آ آ آ آ He set my soul afloat,
آ آ آ آ And drew till day on Daleyï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Bright store of anecdote.

آ آ آ آ He said heï؟½d stick to business,
آ آ آ آ Though he could well be free,
آ آ آ آ If but to save poor devils
آ آ آ آ From harder ï؟½bumsï؟½ than he,
آ آ آ آ Now artist, bard and bailiff
آ آ آ آ Have left this vale of sin,
آ آ آ آ I trust, if they reach Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½ll take that bailiff in.

آ آ آ آ The bards that lived in Manly
آ آ آ آ Have vanished one and one;
آ آ آ آ But do not think in Manly
آ آ آ آ Bohemian days are done.
آ آ آ آ They bled me white in Manly
آ آ آ آ When rich and tempest-tossed,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll leave some bills in Manly
آ آ آ آ To pay for what I lost.

آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d grab and grind in Manly,
آ آ آ آ Then slander, sneer, and flout.
آ آ آ آ The shocked of moral Manly!
آ آ آ آ They starved my brothers out.
آ آ آ آ The miserable village,
آ آ آ آ Set in a scene so fair,
آ آ آ آ Were honester and cleaner
آ آ آ آ If some of us were there!

آ آ آ آ But one went with December,
آ آ آ آ These last lines seem to-night
آ آ آ آ Like some song I remember,
آ آ آ آ And not a song I write.
آ آ آ آ With vision strangely clearer
آ آ آ آ My old chums seem to be,
آ آ آ آ In death and absence, nearer
آ آ آ آ Than eï؟½er they were to me.

آ آ آ آ Alone, and still not lonely,
آ آ آ آ When tears will not be shed,
آ آ آ آ I wish that I could only
آ آ آ آ Believe that they were dead.
آ آ آ آ With hardly curbed emotion,
آ آ آ آ I canï؟½t but think, somehow,
آ آ آ آ In Manly by the ocean
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½re waiting for me now.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
